PARITYQC
# 
Architecture
The ParityQC Architecture, the generalized version of the[**LHZ architecture**](https://parityqc.com/lhz-architecture), provides a fundamentally new way to solve real-world problems on quantum computers, defining both the hardware layout and the algorithms. It is a quantum computing architecture that represents a new pathway to work efficiently with every currently available quantum computing platform, from NISQ towards fully error-corrected devices in the future.
The conventional approach to quantum computing, and in particular quantum optimization, typically requires the implementation of a large number of interactions between different qubits. Such an implementation quickly becomes unfeasible in practice, especially since the structure of these interactions can change from problem to problem. To solve these issues, the ParityQC Architecture establishes an additional level of encoding between the problem and the hardware. With this novel quantum computing architecture, only globally uniform interactions between neighbouring qubits are required. All problem-depending interactions are reduced to local operations which are much easier to control.
![ParityQC Architecture - quantum computing architecture](https://parityqc.com/wp-content/uploads/2021/11/Parity-QC-Architecture-V2.png)
# Reduced Complexity
Building quantum computers is a difficult task. By reducing the complexity of the architecture, the demands on the hardware structure are greatly simplified. This results in a decisive time advantage in the quest for an operational quantum computer. In the most ideal version, the chip consists only of qubits (the white dots in the icon) and interactions between up to four nearest neighbours (the red square). In addition, when building with the ParityQC Architecture, algorithms get simplified as well. Traditionally, the complexity of the problem is encoded in gates between qubits, but with our approach, the problem will be encoded in the local fields of the qubits only. This allows for the design of much simpler algorithms.
![](https://parityqc.com/wp-content/uploads/2021/11/Reduce-Complexity.jpg)
# One Chip For Every Problem
In the ParityQC Architecture, the interactions are independent of the problem. The problem is transferred to the software rather than implemented in the hardware. By separating the problem (from the hardware), we are able to solve any problem with the same chip. This includes combinatorial optimization problems, material simulation, machine learning, quantum chemistry and even[**general quantum algorithms**](https://parityqc.com/universal-parity-quantum-computing) beyond NISQ.
![](https://parityqc.com/wp-content/uploads/2021/11/One-Chip.jpg)
# All Methods and Platforms
The ParityQC Architecture is compatible with all currently available qubit platforms and can offer advantages for every type of hardware manufacturer. This quantum computing architecture provides a fundamentally new set of possibilities for the development of algorithms. It effectively functions as a translator of the problem, independently from the algorithm, which means it is suitable for both digital and quantum annealing applications.
![quantum computing architecture - ParityQC](https://parityqc.com/wp-content/uploads/2021/11/All-Methods.jpg)
# Scalability through Parallelizability
A significant advantage for digital applications is the complete[**parallelizability**](https://parityqc.com/parallelizable-gates) of the quantum gates. Full parallelizability enables[**constant-depth algorithms**](https://parityqc.com/constant-depth-code-deformations-in-the-parity-architecture), a new paradigm in quantum computing. This is a critical factor for the scalability of quantum computers and the great strength of our approach in the medium- and long-term. By introducing parallelizable gates, it is possible to eliminate unwanted cross-talk between qubits. For quantum annealing, we can implement additional quantum algorithmic building blocks (e.g.[**counter-diabatic driving**](https://parityqc.com/non-adiabatic-lhz)) that are challenging traditional methods.
![](https://parityqc.com/wp-content/uploads/2021/11/Scalability.jpg)
# Qubit Control
The technological bottleneck of scalable quantum systems is not the amount of qubits, but instead how these qubits are controlled. Due to its ability to parallelize gates, the ParityQC Architecture introduces algorithms based on[**globally uniform gates**](https://parityqc.com/a-parallelizable-qaoa-with-local-only-interactions). In each step, a pattern of gates are executed at the same time. This removes the need to implement a control signal for each individual gate and only requires ONE single control signal for all gates instead. This provides a huge advantage for the hardware design and a route to mitigate cross-talk errors during qubit design.
![](https://parityqc.com/wp-content/uploads/2021/11/Quibt-Control.jpg)
# Error correction
Due to the redundant encoding of the logical qubits in the ParityQC Architecture, the resulting constraints can be used for error mitigation and error correction. Among other applications, the encoding can be combined with the error suppression capabilities of noise-biased qubits for **[fault tolerant quantum computing](https://parityqc.com/fault-tolerant-quantum-computing-with-the-parity-code-and-noise-biased-qubits "fault tolerant quantum computing")** , or it can be used to enhance the solution quality of optimization algorithms through[**error mitigation**](https://parityqc.com/error-mitigation-for-quantum-approximate-optimization) of the final readout.
![](https://parityqc.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/01/Error-Correction.jpg)

				<span class="block opacity-0 subtitle uppercase font-bold text-xs lg:text-lg text-parity-red tracking-widexl py-5">PARITYQC</span>
		
				<h1 class="post-intro opacity-0 split text-4xl lg:text-8xl flex-col column mb-7"><div style="position:relative;display:inline-block;" class="overflow-hidden"><div style="position:relative;display:inline-block;" class="transform translate-y-[150%] align-top">Architecture</div></div></h1>
		
	</div>


</div>

<div class="container text-center max-w-screen-lg mx-auto pt-12 pb-16 px-4">

	<div class="opacity-0 post-content prose md:prose-lg xl:prose-xl max-w-none text-white text-left block-content">
		<p>The ParityQC Architecture, the generalized version of the<b> </b><a href="https://parityqc.com/lhz-architecture"><b>LHZ architecture</b></a>, provides a fundamentally new way to solve real-world problems on quantum computers, defining both the hardware layout and the algorithms. It is a quantum computing architecture that represents a new pathway to work efficiently with every currently available quantum computing platform, from NISQ towards fully error-corrected devices in the future.</p>
<p>The conventional approach to quantum computing, and in particular quantum optimization, typically requires the implementation of a large number of interactions between different qubits. Such an implementation quickly becomes unfeasible in practice, especially since the structure of these interactions can change from problem to problem. To solve these issues, the ParityQC Architecture establishes an additional level of encoding between the problem and the hardware. With this novel quantum computing architecture, only globally uniform interactions between neighbouring qubits are required. All problem-depending interactions are reduced to local operations which are much easier to control.</p>
<p> </p>
<p style="text-align: center;"><img fetchpriority="high" decoding="async" class="aligncenter wp-image-647 size-full" src="https://parityqc.com/wp-content/uploads/2021/11/Parity-QC-Architecture-V2.png" alt="ParityQC Architecture - quantum computing architecture" width="700" height="406" srcset="https://parityqc.com/wp-content/uploads/2021/11/Parity-QC-Architecture-V2.png 700w, https://parityqc.com/wp-content/uploads/2021/11/Parity-QC-Architecture-V2-300x174.png 300w, https://parityqc.com/wp-content/uploads/2021/11/Parity-QC-Architecture-V2-570x331.png 570w" sizes="(max-width: 700px) 100vw, 700px"></p>
	</div>

	
</div>





<div class="blocks bg-parity-blue text-white">
		
	<div class="grid grid-cols-1 md:grid-cols-2 border-b border-transparent">

				<div class="md:order-1 md:border-r border-b md:border-b-0 border-transparent px-4 py-8 md:px-14 flex flex-col items-start justify-center">
			<div class="prose max-w-none text-current">
				<h1><span style="color: #ffffff;">Reduced Complexity</span></h1>
<p>Building quantum computers is a difficult task. By reducing the complexity of the architecture, the demands on the hardware structure are greatly simplified. This results in a decisive time advantage in the quest for an operational quantum computer. In the most ideal version, the chip consists only of qubits (the white dots in the icon) and interactions between up to four nearest neighbours (the red square). In addition, when building with the ParityQC Architecture, algorithms get simplified as well. Traditionally, the complexity of the problem is encoded in gates between qubits, but with our approach, the problem will be encoded in the local fields of the qubits only. This allows for the design of much simpler algorithms.</p>
			</div>
					</div>
		
				<div class="md:order-2 flex items-center">
			<img class="object-cover w-full h-full" src="https://parityqc.com/wp-content/uploads/2021/11/Reduce-Complexity.jpg" alt="">
		</div>
		
	</div>
		
	<div class="grid grid-cols-1 md:grid-cols-2 border-b border-transparent">

				<div class="md:order-2 md:border-l border-b md:border-b-0 border-transparent px-4 py-8 md:px-14 flex flex-col items-start justify-center">
			<div class="prose max-w-none text-current">
				<h1><span style="color: #ffffff;">One Chip For Every Problem</span></h1>
<p>In the ParityQC Architecture, the interactions are independent of the problem. The problem is transferred to the software rather than implemented in the hardware. By separating the problem (from the hardware), we are able to solve any problem with the same chip. This includes combinatorial optimization problems, material simulation, machine learning, quantum chemistry and even<b> </b><a href="https://parityqc.com/universal-parity-quantum-computing"><b>general quantum algorithms</b></a> beyond NISQ.<span class="Apple-converted-space"> </span></p>
			</div>
					</div>
		
				<div class="md:order-1 flex items-center">
			<img class="object-cover w-full h-full" src="https://parityqc.com/wp-content/uploads/2021/11/One-Chip.jpg" alt="">
		</div>
		
	</div>
		
	<div class="grid grid-cols-1 md:grid-cols-2 border-b border-transparent">

				<div class="md:order-1 md:border-r border-b md:border-b-0 border-transparent px-4 py-8 md:px-14 flex flex-col items-start justify-center">
			<div class="prose max-w-none text-current">
				<h1><span style="color: #ffffff;">All Methods and Platforms</span></h1>
<p>The ParityQC Architecture is compatible with all currently available qubit platforms and can offer advantages for every type of hardware manufacturer. This quantum computing architecture provides a fundamentally new set of possibilities for the development of algorithms. It effectively functions as a translator of the problem, independently from the algorithm, which means it is suitable for both digital and quantum annealing applications.</p>
			</div>
					</div>
		
				<div class="md:order-2 flex items-center">
			<img class="object-cover w-full h-full" src="https://parityqc.com/wp-content/uploads/2021/11/All-Methods.jpg" alt="quantum computing architecture - ParityQC">
		</div>
		
	</div>
		
	<div class="grid grid-cols-1 md:grid-cols-2 border-b border-transparent">

				<div class="md:order-2 md:border-l border-b md:border-b-0 border-transparent px-4 py-8 md:px-14 flex flex-col items-start justify-center">
			<div class="prose max-w-none text-current">
				<h1><span style="color: #ffffff;">Scalability through Parallelizability</span></h1>
<p>A significant advantage for digital applications is the complete<b> </b><a href="https://parityqc.com/parallelizable-gates"><b>parallelizability</b></a> of the quantum gates. Full parallelizability enables<b> </b><a href="https://parityqc.com/constant-depth-code-deformations-in-the-parity-architecture"><b>constant-depth algorithms</b></a>, a new paradigm in quantum computing. This is a critical factor for the scalability of quantum computers and the great strength of our approach in the medium- and long-term. By introducing parallelizable gates, it is possible to eliminate unwanted cross-talk between qubits. For quantum annealing, we can implement additional quantum algorithmic building blocks (e.g.<a href="https://parityqc.com/non-adiabatic-lhz"><b> counter-diabatic driving</b></a>) that are challenging traditional methods.</p>
			</div>
					</div>
		
				<div class="md:order-1 flex items-center">
			<img class="object-cover w-full h-full" src="https://parityqc.com/wp-content/uploads/2021/11/Scalability.jpg" alt="">
		</div>
		
	</div>
		
	<div class="grid grid-cols-1 md:grid-cols-2 border-b border-transparent">

				<div class="md:order-1 md:border-r border-b md:border-b-0 border-transparent px-4 py-8 md:px-14 flex flex-col items-start justify-center">
			<div class="prose max-w-none text-current">
				<h1><span style="color: #ffffff;">Qubit Control</span></h1>
<p>The technological bottleneck of scalable quantum systems is not the amount of qubits, but instead how these qubits are controlled. Due to its ability to parallelize gates, the ParityQC Architecture introduces algorithms based on<b> </b><a href="https://parityqc.com/a-parallelizable-qaoa-with-local-only-interactions"><b>globally uniform gates</b></a>. In each step, a pattern of gates are executed at the same time. This removes the need to implement a control signal for each individual gate and only requires ONE single control signal for all gates instead. This provides a huge advantage for the hardware design and a route to mitigate cross-talk errors during qubit design.</p>
			</div>
					</div>
		
				<div class="md:order-2 flex items-center">
			<img class="object-cover w-full h-full" src="https://parityqc.com/wp-content/uploads/2021/11/Quibt-Control.jpg" alt="">
		</div>
		
	</div>
		
	<div class="grid grid-cols-1 md:grid-cols-2 border-b border-transparent">

				<div class="md:order-2 md:border-l border-b md:border-b-0 border-transparent px-4 py-8 md:px-14 flex flex-col items-start justify-center">
			<div class="prose max-w-none text-current">
				<h1><span style="color: #ffffff;">Error correction</span></h1>
<p>Due to the redundant encoding of the logical qubits in the ParityQC Architecture, the resulting constraints can be used for error mitigation and error correction. Among other applications, the encoding can be combined with the error suppression capabilities of noise-biased qubits for <strong><a title="fault tolerant quantum computing" href="https://parityqc.com/fault-tolerant-quantum-computing-with-the-parity-code-and-noise-biased-qubits" target="_blank" rel="noopener" data-saferedirecturl="https://www.google.com/url?q=https://parityqc.com/fault-tolerant-quantum-computing-with-the-parity-code-and-noise-biased-qubits&source=gmail&ust=1737623255281000&usg=AOvVaw1ODNvqhhA7LHLK7uE0Svyy">fault tolerant quantum computing</a></strong>, or it can be used to enhance the solution quality of optimization algorithms through<b> </b><a href="https://parityqc.com/error-mitigation-for-quantum-approximate-optimization" target="_blank" rel="noopener" data-saferedirecturl="https://www.google.com/url?q=https://parityqc.com/error-mitigation-for-quantum-approximate-optimization&source=gmail&ust=1737623255281000&usg=AOvVaw37XnFSiMo7Fj5xo_yfW4gQ"><b>error mitigation</b></a> of the final readout.</p>
			</div>
					</div>
		
				<div class="md:order-1 flex items-center">
			<img class="object-cover w-full h-full" src="https://parityqc.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/01/Error-Correction.jpg" alt="">
		</div>
		
	</div>
		
</div>

			var answer = document.getElementById(id);
			var icon = btn.querySelector('.parity-faq-icon');
			var isOpen = answer.style.maxHeight && answer.style.maxHeight !== '0px';
			if (isOpen) {
				answer.style.maxHeight = '0';
				icon.textContent = '+';
				btn.setAttribute('aria-expanded', 'false');
			} else {
				answer.style.maxHeight = answer.scrollHeight + 'px';
				icon.textContent = '\u2013';
				btn.setAttribute('aria-expanded', 'true');
			}
		}
	</script>
